docs.text:
Experimental site

This is an experimental documentation site with AI-generated draft content. It is not the official AICR documentation. Content here has not been fully reviewed and may contain errors. The official site is docs.aicr.ai.

Welcome to the documentation for the Massachusetts AI Compute Resource (AICR) — a multi-institutional GPU cluster for AI research, serving Boston University, Harvard, MIT, Northeastern, UMass (five campuses), and Yale. Part of the Massachusetts AI Hub, housed at the MGHPCC in Holyoke, MA.

docs.title: Distributed Training
docs.text:
When a single GPU is not enough -- because your model does not fit in memory or training takes too long -- you need to distribute work across multiple GPUs or nodes. This page helps you choose the right parallelism strategy and provides job scripts for each approach on AICR.

For PyTorch-specific launch commands (torchrun, process group setup), see PyTorch on AICR. For GPU hardware and NCCL details, see Multi-GPU Computing.

docs.title: When to Scale
docs.text:
Consider distributed training when:

  • Training is too slow on one GPU. A single epoch takes hours, and you have many epochs to run. Distributing across GPUs gives near-linear speedup with DDP.
  • The model does not fit in one GPU's memory. Even after reducing batch size and enabling mixed precision, the model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states exceed GPU memory.
  • The dataset is very large. Parallel data loading and processing across multiple GPUs accelerates time-to-solution.

Tip

Before scaling, make sure you have optimized single-GPU performance first: enable mixed precision (torch.amp), use torch.compile, and tune your DataLoader. See PyTorch performance tips.

docs.title: Decision Tree
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    Start["Model to train"] --> Fits{"Model fits on<br/>one GPU?"}
    Fits -- Yes --> Speed{"Need faster<br/>training?"}
    Speed -- Yes --> DDP["Use DDP"]
    Speed -- No --> Single["Single GPU is fine"]
    Fits -- No --> Size{"Fits when sharded<br/>across GPUs?"}
    Size -- Yes --> FSDP["Use FSDP"]
    Size -- No --> Pipeline["Pipeline + Tensor<br/>Parallelism"]
Situation Strategy Model fits on one GPU, want faster training DDP -- replicate model, split data Model fits on one GPU, want to train on more data DDP -- same approach Model does not fit on one GPU FSDP -- shard model across GPUs Very large model (tens of billions of parameters) Pipeline or Tensor Parallelism

docs.title: DDP (Distributed Data Parallel)
docs.text:
The most common and simplest approach. Each GPU holds a complete copy of the model and processes a different shard of the data. Gradients are averaged (all-reduced) across GPUs after each backward pass.

When to use: The model fits in a single GPU's memory. You want to speed up training by processing more data in parallel.

Key properties:

  • Near-linear scaling up to dozens of GPUs
  • Each GPU needs enough memory for the full model, a batch of data, gradients, and optimizer states
  • Communication cost is one all-reduce per backward pass

For DDP job scripts (single-node and multi-node), see PyTorch on AICR. The key commands are:

  • Single node: torchrun --nproc_per_node=8 train.py
  • Multi-node: srun torchrun --nnodes=$SLURM_NNODES --nproc_per_node=8 ... train.py

Your training script must wrap the model in torch.nn.parallel.DistributedDataParallel. See the PyTorch Training recipe for a complete working example.

Note

AICR's NDR400 InfiniBand interconnect (400 Gb/s) keeps inter-node gradient synchronization fast. NCCL uses this automatically when available.

docs.title: FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel)
docs.text:
FSDP shards model parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across GPUs. Each GPU only holds a fraction of the model. During forward and backward passes, parameters are gathered on-the-fly and released after use.

When to use: Your model does not fit on a single GPU, but it fits when sharded across the available GPUs.

Key properties:

  • Dramatically reduces per-GPU memory usage
  • Allows training models that are 4--8x larger than what one GPU can hold
  • Higher communication overhead than DDP (all-gather + reduce-scatter per layer)

docs.title: FSDP Job Script (b200-batch)
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=fsdp_training
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --nodes=2
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=1
#SBATCH --gpus-per-node=8
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=64
#SBATCH --mem=0
#SBATCH --time=24:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
source ~/pytorch-env/bin/activate

export MASTER_ADDR=$(scontrol show hostnames $SLURM_JOB_NODELIST | head -n 1)
export MASTER_PORT=29500

srun torchrun \
    --nnodes=$SLURM_NNODES \
    --nproc_per_node=8 \
    --rdzv_id=$SLURM_JOB_ID \
    --rdzv_backend=c10d \
    --rdzv_endpoint=$MASTER_ADDR:$MASTER_PORT \
    train_fsdp.py

In your training script, wrap the model with FSDP:

from torch.distributed.fsdp import FullyShardedDataParallel as FSDP
from torch.distributed.fsdp import ShardingStrategy

model = FSDP(
    model,
    sharding_strategy=ShardingStrategy.FULL_SHARD,
    use_orig_params=True,
)

FSDP + mixed precision

Combine FSDP with BF16 mixed precision for best memory efficiency on B200 GPUs:

from torch.distributed.fsdp import MixedPrecision

bf16_policy = MixedPrecision(
    param_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    reduce_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    buffer_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
)

model = FSDP(model, mixed_precision=bf16_policy)

docs.title: Pipeline Parallelism
docs.text:
Pipeline parallelism splits the model into sequential stages, with each stage assigned to a different GPU (or group of GPUs). Data flows through the stages in a pipeline, so multiple micro-batches are in flight simultaneously.

When to use: Very large sequential models (e.g., deep Transformers) where FSDP alone is not sufficient, or when you want to overlap computation across stages.

Key properties:

  • Requires splitting the model into roughly equal stages
  • Pipeline bubbles reduce efficiency; more micro-batches reduce bubble overhead
  • Often combined with DDP or FSDP within each stage

PyTorch provides torch.distributed.pipelining for pipeline parallelism. This is an advanced technique; consult the PyTorch pipeline parallelism documentation for implementation details.

docs.title: Tensor Parallelism
docs.text:
Tensor parallelism splits individual layers (e.g., large linear layers in Transformers) across GPUs. Each GPU computes part of each layer and synchronizes at layer boundaries.

When to use: Single layers are too large for one GPU's memory, or you are working with very wide Transformer models.

Key properties:

  • Requires very high-bandwidth GPU-to-GPU communication (works best within a single node via NVLink)
  • Typically combined with pipeline parallelism across nodes and DDP for data parallelism
  • Libraries like Megatron-LM and DeepSpeed implement this automatically

Warning

Tensor parallelism across nodes (over InfiniBand rather than NVLink) incurs significant latency. Prefer tensor parallelism within a node and pipeline parallelism across nodes.

docs.title: Strategy Summary
docs.text: Strategy Model Fits on 1 GPU? Communication Complexity Scaling DDP Yes All-reduce (gradients) Low Near-linear FSDP Sharded, yes All-gather + reduce-scatter Medium Good Pipeline No Point-to-point (activations) High Good with many micro-batches Tensor No (layer-level) All-reduce (partial results) High Within-node best

docs.title: Combining Strategies
docs.text:
Large-scale training often uses multiple strategies simultaneously. A common pattern:

  • Tensor parallelism within a node (8 GPUs connected by NVLink)
  • Pipeline parallelism across nodes (connected by InfiniBand)
  • Data parallelism (DDP) across pipeline-parallel replicas

Libraries such as Megatron-LM, DeepSpeed, and Hugging Face Accelerate can orchestrate these combinations.

docs.title: AICR's Interconnect
docs.text:
AICR's B200 nodes have a dedicated compute InfiniBand fabric (NDR400, 400 Gb/s) optimized for GPU-to-GPU collective operations via NCCL. This makes multi-node distributed training fast -- gradient synchronization happens over InfiniBand rather than Ethernet.

For hardware-level details, see Multi-GPU Computing.

docs.title: Experiment Tracking
docs.text: Experiment tracking tools help you log hyperparameters, metrics, and artifacts so you can compare runs, reproduce results, and collaborate with your team. AICR supports Weights & Biases (W&B), MLflow, and TensorBoard.

docs.title: Why Track Experiments
docs.text:
Training ML models involves many moving parts: learning rates, architectures, data splits, random seeds. Without systematic tracking, it is easy to lose track of which configuration produced which result.

Experiment tracking tools solve this by:

  • Logging automatically -- record hyperparameters, metrics, and system info for every run
  • Comparing runs -- view performance across experiments in tables and charts
  • Reproducing results -- store the exact configuration needed to recreate any run
  • Collaborating -- share dashboards and results with your team

docs.title: Weights & Biases (W&B)
docs.text: Weights & Biases is a hosted experiment tracking platform. It provides dashboards, hyperparameter sweep orchestration, and artifact versioning.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Install W&B in your Conda environment or venv:

$ pip install wandb

docs.title: API Key Configuration
docs.text:
W&B requires an API key to authenticate. Get your key from wandb.ai/authorize, then configure it so batch jobs can authenticate without interactive login.

Store your key in a file readable only by you:

$ echo "YOUR_API_KEY" > ~/.wandb_api_key
$ chmod 600 ~/.wandb_api_key

Reference it in your job scripts:

export WANDB_API_KEY=$(cat ~/.wandb_api_key)

Warning

Never hardcode your API key in job scripts or source code. Use the file-based approach above or set the key in your ~/.bashrc.

Compute nodes have outbound internet

AICR compute nodes can reach external services (W&B, HuggingFace, PyPI, etc.) but do not accept inbound connections from outside the cluster.

docs.title: Logging Metrics in a Training Script
docs.text:
import wandb

wandb.init(
    project="my_project",
    config={
        "learning_rate": 1e-4,
        "batch_size": 32,
        "epochs": 50,
        "architecture": "resnet50",
    },
)

for epoch in range(50):
    train_loss = train_one_epoch(model, train_loader)
    val_loss, val_acc = evaluate(model, val_loader)

    wandb.log({
        "epoch": epoch,
        "train/loss": train_loss,
        "val/loss": val_loss,
        "val/accuracy": val_acc,
    })

wandb.finish()

docs.title: Batch Job with W&B
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=train_wandb
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=32G
#SBATCH --time=08:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load cuda

eval "$(conda shell.bash hook)"
conda activate myenv

export WANDB_API_KEY=$(cat ~/.wandb_api_key)

python train.py

docs.title: Viewing Results
docs.text: After your job runs, view results in the W&B web dashboard at wandb.ai. The dashboard shows metric plots, system utilization, hyperparameter comparisons, and run logs.

docs.title: W&B Offline Mode
docs.text:
If you prefer not to send data from compute nodes (or if the network is temporarily unavailable), use offline mode. W&B writes all logs locally, and you sync them from a login node afterward:

export WANDB_MODE=offline

After the job completes, sync from a login node:

$ wandb sync /scratch/$USER/wandb/offline-run-*

Tip

Set WANDB_DIR=/scratch/$USER/wandb in your job script to write W&B logs to scratch instead of your home directory. W&B run directories can grow large.

docs.title: W&B Sweeps with Job Arrays
docs.text:
Combine W&B sweeps with Slurm job arrays for hyperparameter searches:

  1. Create a sweep configuration:

    # sweep.yaml
program: train.py
method: bayes
metric:
  name: val/loss
  goal: minimize
parameters:
  learning_rate:
    min: 0.00001
    max: 0.01
  batch_size:
    values: [16, 32, 64, 128]
  dropout:
    min: 0.1
    max: 0.5

  2. Initialize the sweep from a login node:

    $ wandb sweep sweep.yaml

    This prints a sweep ID like USERNAME/PROJECT/SWEEP_ID.

  3. Submit a job array where each task runs a sweep agent:

    #!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=wandb_sweep
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=32G
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --array=0-9
#SBATCH --output=%x-%a-%j.out

module purge
module load cuda

eval "$(conda shell.bash hook)"
conda activate myenv

export WANDB_API_KEY=$(cat ~/.wandb_api_key)

wandb agent USERNAME/PROJECT/SWEEP_ID

Each array task launches a sweep agent that pulls configurations from the W&B server and runs trials until the sweep is complete or the job time expires.

docs.title: MLflow
docs.text: MLflow is an open-source experiment tracking tool that can run entirely locally without a hosted service.

docs.title: Local Tracking Server
docs.text:
Start an MLflow tracking server on scratch:

$ pip install mlflow
$ mlflow server \
    --backend-store-uri sqlite:///scratch/$USER/mlflow/mlflow.db \
    --default-artifact-root /scratch/$USER/mlflow/artifacts \
    --host 0.0.0.0 \
    --port 5000

Access the MLflow UI through an SSH tunnel:

$ ssh -L 5000:NODE_NAME:5000 USERNAME@login.aicr.ai

Then open http://localhost:5000 in your browser.

docs.title: Logging with MLflow
docs.text:
import mlflow

mlflow.set_tracking_uri("http://localhost:5000")
mlflow.set_experiment("my_experiment")

with mlflow.start_run():
    mlflow.log_params({
        "learning_rate": 1e-4,
        "batch_size": 32,
    })

    for epoch in range(50):
        train_loss = train_one_epoch(model, train_loader)
        mlflow.log_metric("train_loss", train_loss, step=epoch)

    mlflow.log_artifact("model.pt")

Note

MLflow does not require internet access. All data stays on the cluster, which may be preferred for sensitive research data.

docs.title: TensorBoard
docs.text: TensorBoard provides visualization for training runs. It works with PyTorch, TensorFlow, and JAX.

docs.title: Writing TensorBoard Logs
docs.text:
With PyTorch:

import os
from torch.utils.tensorboard import SummaryWriter

log_dir = os.path.expandvars("/scratch/$USER/tb_logs/run_01")
writer = SummaryWriter(log_dir=log_dir)

for epoch in range(50):
    train_loss = train_one_epoch(model, train_loader)
    writer.add_scalar("train/loss", train_loss, epoch)
    writer.add_scalar("val/accuracy", val_acc, epoch)

writer.close()

docs.title: Viewing TensorBoard via SSH Tunnel
docs.text:
Start TensorBoard on a login node or inside an interactive job:

$ tensorboard --logdir=/scratch/$USER/tb_logs --port=6006 --bind_all

Set up an SSH tunnel from your local machine:

$ ssh -L 6006:NODE_NAME:6006 USERNAME@login.aicr.ai

Open http://localhost:6006 in your browser.

Tip

Point --logdir at a parent directory containing multiple run subdirectories. TensorBoard will display all runs together for easy comparison.

docs.title: Storage Best Practices
docs.text:
Experiment logs, checkpoints, and artifacts can consume significant space. Follow these guidelines:

  • Write logs to scratch, not home -- set WANDB_DIR, MLflow artifact roots, and TensorBoard log_dir to paths under /scratch/$USER/
  • Clean up finished experiments -- delete logs for runs you no longer need
  • Archive important results to work -- copy final metrics and key artifacts to /work/INSTITUTION/ for longer-term storage
export WANDB_DIR=/scratch/$USER/wandb
export MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI=sqlite:///scratch/$USER/mlflow/mlflow.db

Warning

Scratch is subject to regular purging. After your experiments are complete, copy important logs and artifacts to /work/INSTITUTION/ or off-cluster before they are removed.

docs.title: Hugging Face on AICR
docs.text: Hugging Face provides a large ecosystem of pre-trained models, datasets, and training utilities. This page covers how to configure the cache, download models, run inference with pipelines, fine-tune with the Trainer API, and scale to multiple GPUs with Accelerate on AICR.

docs.title: Setting Up the Cache
docs.text:
By default, Hugging Face downloads models and datasets to ~/.cache/huggingface, which counts against your 100 GB home quota. Large models (7B+ parameters) can be 15--140 GB each. Redirect the cache to scratch:

Add to your ~/.bashrc:

export HF_HOME=/scratch/$USER/.cache/huggingface

Then reload:

$ source ~/.bashrc

Warning

Files on /scratch are not backed up and may be subject to purge policies. Keep a record of which models you have downloaded so you can re-download them if needed.

docs.title: Small Models (Login Node)
docs.text:
For small models (under a few GB), downloading on the login node is acceptable:

$ pip install huggingface_hub
$ huggingface-cli download bert-base-uncased

docs.title: Large Models (Compute Node)
docs.text:
For large models, download on a compute node to avoid overloading the login node. Request a short interactive session:

$ salloc --partition=cpu --time=02:00:00 --mem=32G --account=ACCOUNT_NAME

Then download:

$ huggingface-cli download meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct

Tip

For gated models (e.g., Llama), you need a Hugging Face access token. Create one at huggingface.co/settings/tokens and log in:

$ huggingface-cli login

The token is stored in $HF_HOME/token. Do not share it or commit it to version control.

docs.title: Downloading Specific Files
docs.text:
If you only need certain files from a model (e.g., just the tokenizer or a specific checkpoint):

$ huggingface-cli download meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct --include "tokenizer*"

docs.title: Pipeline API (Quick Inference)
docs.text:
The pipeline API is the fastest way to run inference with a pre-trained model:

from transformers import pipeline

classifier = pipeline("sentiment-analysis", device=0)
result = classifier("AICR is a great cluster for AI research.")
print(result)
# [{'label': 'POSITIVE', 'score': 0.9998}]

For text generation:

from transformers import pipeline

generator = pipeline(
    "text-generation",
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    device_map="auto",
    torch_dtype="bfloat16",
)

output = generator("Explain what a GPU is in one sentence:", max_new_tokens=50)
print(output[0]["generated_text"])

Note

Setting device_map="auto" distributes the model across all available GPUs automatically. This is convenient but less efficient than explicit tensor parallelism via vLLM. For production inference, see Inference and Serving.

docs.title: Fine-Tuning with Trainer
docs.text:
The Hugging Face Trainer class handles training loops, logging, checkpointing, and evaluation. Here is a job script for fine-tuning a model on AICR:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=hf-finetune
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=64G
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
source ~/hf-env/bin/activate

export HF_HOME=/scratch/$USER/.cache/huggingface

python finetune.py

Example finetune.py:

import os
from transformers import (
    AutoModelForSequenceClassification,
    AutoTokenizer,
    Trainer,
    TrainingArguments,
)
from datasets import load_dataset

model_name = "bert-base-uncased"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(model_name, num_labels=2)

dataset = load_dataset("imdb")

def tokenize(batch):
    return tokenizer(batch["text"], padding="max_length", truncation=True, max_length=512)

tokenized = dataset.map(tokenize, batched=True)

training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir=os.path.expandvars("/scratch/$USER/hf-checkpoints/imdb"),
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=16,
    per_device_eval_batch_size=64,
    eval_strategy="epoch",
    save_strategy="epoch",
    logging_steps=100,
    bf16=True,
    dataloader_num_workers=4,
    dataloader_pin_memory=True,
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=tokenized["train"],
    eval_dataset=tokenized["test"],
)

trainer.train()
trainer.save_model(os.path.expandvars("/scratch/$USER/hf-models/imdb-classifier"))

docs.title: Multi-GPU Training with Accelerate
docs.text: Hugging Face Accelerate is a lightweight library that enables multi-GPU training with minimal code changes. It works with both the Trainer API and custom training loops.

docs.title: Configure Accelerate
docs.text:
Generate a configuration file:

$ accelerate config

Answer the prompts for your setup (multi-GPU, single node, BF16, etc.). The configuration is saved to ~/.cache/huggingface/accelerate/default_config.yaml.

docs.title: Multi-GPU Job Script with Accelerate
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=hf-accelerate
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gpus-per-node=8
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=64
#SBATCH --mem=0
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
source ~/hf-env/bin/activate

export HF_HOME=/scratch/$USER/.cache/huggingface

accelerate launch \
    --num_processes=8 \
    --mixed_precision=bf16 \
    finetune.py

Tip

The Trainer class automatically detects Accelerate and uses it for distributed training. You can use the same finetune.py script shown above -- Trainer handles multi-GPU data distribution and gradient synchronization.

docs.title: Multi-Node with Accelerate
docs.text:
For multi-node training, combine srun with Accelerate:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=hf-multinode
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --nodes=2
#SBATCH --gpus-per-node=8
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=64
#SBATCH --mem=0
#SBATCH --time=24:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
source ~/hf-env/bin/activate

export HF_HOME=/scratch/$USER/.cache/huggingface
export MASTER_ADDR=$(scontrol show hostnames $SLURM_JOB_NODELIST | head -n 1)
export MASTER_PORT=29500

srun accelerate launch \
    --num_processes=16 \
    --num_machines=2 \
    --mixed_precision=bf16 \
    --main_process_ip=$MASTER_ADDR \
    --main_process_port=$MASTER_PORT \
    finetune.py

docs.title: Cache Management
docs.text:
Over time, cached models and datasets accumulate. Monitor and clean up:

# Check cache size
$ du -sh /scratch/$USER/.cache/huggingface/

# List cached models
$ huggingface-cli scan-cache

# Delete specific cached models
$ huggingface-cli delete-cache

Tip

The huggingface-cli scan-cache command shows each cached repo, its size, and last access time. Use huggingface-cli delete-cache interactively to select which repos to remove.

docs.title: Sharing Models Across a Group
docs.text:
If multiple members of your research group use the same models, consider downloading once to a shared location:

$ export HF_HOME=/scratch/GROUP_NAME/shared-hf-cache
$ huggingface-cli download meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct

Then each group member can point HF_HOME to the shared cache. Coordinate to avoid duplicate downloads.

docs.title: Inference and Serving
docs.text: Running inference on large models -- especially large language models (LLMs) -- often requires multiple GPUs. This page covers when multi-GPU inference is needed, how to use vLLM for high-throughput serving on AICR, and batch vs. interactive inference patterns.

docs.title: When Multi-GPU Inference Is Needed
docs.text:
A single GPU can run inference on many models. Multi-GPU inference becomes necessary when:

  • The model does not fit in one GPU's memory. A 70B-parameter model in FP16 requires approximately 140 GB of GPU memory, exceeding a single GPU.
  • You need higher throughput. Serving many concurrent requests benefits from distributing work across GPUs.
  • Latency requirements are strict. Tensor parallelism splits computation across GPUs, reducing time per token.

Tip

Before using multiple GPUs, try quantization (AWQ, GPTQ) to fit the model on fewer GPUs. A 70B model quantized to 4-bit may fit on 2 GPUs instead of 4.

docs.title: vLLM Overview
docs.text:
vLLM is a high-throughput inference engine for LLMs. It uses PagedAttention to manage GPU memory efficiently and supports tensor parallelism for multi-GPU serving.

Key features:

  • PagedAttention -- reduces memory waste from KV cache fragmentation
  • Continuous batching -- dynamically batches incoming requests for high throughput
  • Tensor parallelism -- splits model layers across GPUs on a single node
  • Broad model support -- Llama, Mistral, Qwen, Phi, Gemma, and many more

docs.title: Container (Recommended)
docs.text:
The vLLM container from Docker Hub includes all dependencies and is the simplest way to get started:

$ apptainer pull /scratch/$USER/containers/vllm.sif docker://vllm/vllm-openai:latest

Note

The vLLM container is large (10+ GB). Pull it on a compute node via salloc rather than a login node, and store it on /scratch/$USER.

docs.title: pip
docs.text:
If you prefer a Conda or venv environment:

$ conda create --prefix /scratch/$USER/conda-envs/vllm python=3.12
$ conda activate /scratch/$USER/conda-envs/vllm
$ pip install vllm

docs.title: Batch Inference
docs.text: Batch inference processes a list of prompts in a single job. This is the most common pattern for research workloads: generate completions for a dataset, run evaluations, or extract embeddings.

docs.title: Single-GPU Batch Inference
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=vllm-batch
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=64G
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge

apptainer exec --nv /scratch/$USER/containers/vllm.sif \
    python batch_inference.py

Example batch_inference.py:

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

llm = LLM(model="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct")
sampling_params = SamplingParams(temperature=0.7, max_tokens=512)

prompts = [
    "Explain the significance of attention mechanisms in transformers.",
    "What are the main challenges in protein structure prediction?",
    "Summarize the key ideas behind reinforcement learning from human feedback.",
]

outputs = llm.generate(prompts, sampling_params)

for output in outputs:
    print(f"Prompt: {output.prompt[:60]}...")
    print(f"Output: {output.outputs[0].text[:200]}")
    print("---")

docs.title: Multi-GPU Batch Inference (Tensor Parallelism)
docs.text:
For models that do not fit on one GPU, use tensor parallelism. This example serves a 70B model across 4 B200 GPUs:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=vllm-multi-gpu
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gpus-per-node=4
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=32
#SBATCH --mem=256G
#SBATCH --time=08:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge

export HF_HOME=/scratch/$USER/.cache/huggingface

apptainer exec --nv /scratch/$USER/containers/vllm.sif \
    python batch_inference_tp.py

In batch_inference_tp.py, set tensor_parallel_size to match your GPU count:

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

llm = LLM(
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct",
    tensor_parallel_size=4,
    dtype="bfloat16",
)

sampling_params = SamplingParams(temperature=0.7, max_tokens=512)

prompts = ["Your prompts here..."]
outputs = llm.generate(prompts, sampling_params)

for output in outputs:
    print(output.outputs[0].text)

Tip

When using all 8 B200 GPUs on a node, set tensor_parallel_size=8 and request --gpus-per-node=8 with --mem=0 for all available memory.

docs.title: Interactive Serving
docs.text: vLLM can also run as an OpenAI-compatible API server, useful for interactive exploration or when multiple users or scripts need to query the same model.

docs.title: Serving Job Script (4 B200 GPUs)
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=vllm-server
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gpus-per-node=4
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=32
#SBATCH --mem=256G
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge

export HF_HOME=/scratch/$USER/.cache/huggingface

apptainer exec --nv /scratch/$USER/containers/vllm.sif \
    python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
        --model meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct \
        --tensor-parallel-size 4 \
        --dtype bfloat16 \
        --host 0.0.0.0 \
        --port 8000

Query the server from another terminal (or from the same node):

$ curl http://NODE_NAME:8000/v1/completions \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
      "model": "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct",
      "prompt": "The capital of France is",
      "max_tokens": 50
    }'

Replace NODE_NAME with the compute node hostname (find it with squeue --me).

Warning

The vLLM server listens on a compute node, which is only reachable from within the AICR network. Use an SSH tunnel if you need to access it from your local machine:

$ ssh -L 8000:NODE_NAME:8000 USERNAME@login.aicr.ai

docs.title: Batch vs. Interactive: Which to Choose
docs.text:
Approach Best For Advantages Batch inference Processing a fixed dataset of prompts Efficient resource use, no idle GPU time Interactive serving Exploratory work, multiple users querying one model Persistent endpoint, OpenAI-compatible API

For most research workflows, batch inference is preferred -- it processes all prompts and releases GPUs when done. Use interactive serving when you need a persistent endpoint or are building an application that queries the model repeatedly.

docs.title: Tensor Parallelism for Large Models
docs.text:
The number of GPUs required depends on model size and precision:

Model Size Precision Approx. Memory GPUs (B200, 192 GB) GPUs (RTX PRO 6000, 96 GB) 7–8B BF16 ~16 GB 1 1 13B BF16 ~26 GB 1 1 34B BF16 ~68 GB 1 1 70B BF16 ~140 GB 1 2 70B 4-bit (AWQ) ~35 GB 1 1 405B BF16 ~810 GB 5+ 9+ (multi-node)

Note

These are approximate values for model weights only. Actual memory usage includes KV cache, activations, and framework overhead. Request more GPUs than the minimum if you need to serve many concurrent requests.

docs.title: JAX on AICR
docs.text: JAX is a high-performance numerical computing library from Google that combines NumPy-like syntax with automatic differentiation and XLA (Accelerated Linear Algebra) compilation. It is widely used for scientific computing, physics-informed neural networks, molecular dynamics, and research that requires custom gradient computation.

docs.title: What Is JAX?
docs.text:
JAX provides three core capabilities:

  • NumPy API -- jax.numpy is a drop-in replacement for NumPy that runs on GPUs
  • Automatic differentiation -- jax.grad differentiates arbitrary Python functions, including higher-order derivatives
  • XLA compilation -- jax.jit compiles functions to optimized GPU kernels via XLA, often outperforming hand-written CUDA

JAX is functional by design: functions are pure (no side effects), and arrays are immutable. This enables aggressive compiler optimizations and straightforward parallelism.

docs.title: pip
docs.text:
Install JAX with CUDA support in a virtual environment:

$ python3 -m venv /scratch/$USER/jax-env
$ source /scratch/$USER/jax-env/bin/activate
$ pip install "jax[cuda12]"

docs.title: Conda
docs.text:
$ conda create --prefix /scratch/$USER/conda-envs/jax python=3.12
$ conda activate /scratch/$USER/conda-envs/jax
$ pip install "jax[cuda12]"

Note

JAX's CUDA wheels are installed via pip even within a Conda environment. The jax[cuda12] extra pulls in jaxlib with CUDA 12 support.

docs.title: Container
docs.text:
Pull the NVIDIA JAX container from NGC:

$ apptainer pull /scratch/$USER/containers/jax.sif docker://nvcr.io/nvidia/jax:24.04-py3

Run with GPU support:

$ apptainer exec --nv /scratch/$USER/containers/jax.sif python my_script.py

docs.title: Verifying GPU Access
docs.text:
Confirm that JAX detects the GPUs allocated to your job:

$ python3 -c "
import jax
print('Devices:', jax.devices())
print('Device count:', jax.device_count())
print('Default backend:', jax.default_backend())
"

Expected output on a B200 node with 8 GPUs:

Devices: [CudaDevice(id=0), CudaDevice(id=1), ..., CudaDevice(id=7)]
Device count: 8
Default backend: gpu

Warning

If jax.devices() returns CPU devices only, verify that you requested GPUs in your Slurm script and that JAX was installed with CUDA support (jax[cuda12]).

docs.title: Single-GPU Example
docs.text:
A simple JAX program that trains a linear regression model:

import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jax import grad, jit

# Generate synthetic data
key = jax.random.PRNGKey(42)
X = jax.random.normal(key, (1000, 10))
true_w = jnp.ones(10)
y = X @ true_w + 0.1 * jax.random.normal(key, (1000,))

# Model: linear regression
def loss_fn(w, X, y):
    pred = X @ w
    return jnp.mean((pred - y) ** 2)

grad_fn = jit(grad(loss_fn))

# Training loop
w = jnp.zeros(10)
lr = 0.01

for step in range(500):
    g = grad_fn(w, X, y)
    w = w - lr * g
    if step % 100 == 0:
        print(f"Step {step}, loss: {loss_fn(w, X, y):.6f}")

docs.title: Single-GPU Job Script
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=jax-single
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=32G
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
source /scratch/$USER/jax-env/bin/activate

python train_jax.py

docs.title: Multi-GPU with JAX
docs.text: JAX supports multi-GPU computation through its sharding API. Unlike PyTorch, JAX does not require launching separate processes per GPU -- a single JAX process manages all GPUs on a node.

docs.title: Single-Node Multi-GPU (Automatic Sharding)
docs.text:
Use jax.sharding to distribute arrays and computation across GPUs:

import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jax.sharding import Mesh, PartitionSpec, NamedSharding

# Create a mesh of devices
devices = jax.devices()
mesh = Mesh(devices, axis_names=("batch",))

# Shard data across GPUs along the batch dimension
sharding = NamedSharding(mesh, PartitionSpec("batch"))

# Create sharded data
data = jax.random.normal(jax.random.PRNGKey(0), (8192, 512))
data = jax.device_put(data, sharding)

# JIT-compiled function runs in parallel across devices
@jax.jit
def process(x):
    return jnp.sin(x) @ jnp.ones((512, 256))

result = process(data)
print(f"Result shape: {result.shape}, sharding: {result.sharding}")

docs.title: Multi-Node with jax.distributed
docs.text:
For multi-node JAX workloads, initialize jax.distributed and use srun to launch one process per node:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=jax-multinode
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --nodes=2
#SBATCH --gpus-per-node=8
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=64
#SBATCH --mem=0
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
source /scratch/$USER/jax-env/bin/activate

export MASTER_ADDR=$(scontrol show hostnames $SLURM_JOB_NODELIST | head -n 1)

srun --mpi=pmix python train_jax_distributed.py

In train_jax_distributed.py, initialize the distributed runtime:

import jax

jax.distributed.initialize()

print(f"Global devices: {jax.device_count()}")
print(f"Local devices: {jax.local_device_count()}")

# Now use jax.sharding.Mesh with all global devices
# Your training code here...

Note

jax.distributed.initialize() uses environment variables set by Slurm and MPI to discover all nodes. After initialization, jax.devices() returns GPUs from all nodes.

docs.title: Common JAX Libraries
docs.text:
JAX has a modular ecosystem. Common companion libraries:

Library Purpose Install Flax Neural network library (the nn.Module of JAX) pip install flax Optax Gradient transformations and optimizers (Adam, SGD, etc.) pip install optax Orbax Checkpointing for JAX models pip install orbax-checkpoint Equinox PyTorch-like neural networks in JAX pip install equinox Diffrax Differential equation solvers pip install diffrax

A typical Flax + Optax training pattern:

import flax.linen as nn
import optax

class MLP(nn.Module):
    hidden_dim: int
    out_dim: int

    @nn.compact
    def __call__(self, x):
        x = nn.Dense(self.hidden_dim)(x)
        x = nn.relu(x)
        x = nn.Dense(self.out_dim)(x)
        return x

model = MLP(hidden_dim=256, out_dim=10)
params = model.init(jax.random.PRNGKey(0), jnp.ones((1, 784)))

optimizer = optax.adam(learning_rate=1e-3)
opt_state = optimizer.init(params)

docs.title: JAX vs. PyTorch
docs.text:
Aspect JAX PyTorch Programming model Functional (pure functions, immutable arrays) Imperative (in-place ops, mutable tensors) Compilation XLA (jax.jit) -- compiles entire functions torch.compile -- optional, graph capture Autodiff jax.grad on any Python function loss.backward() on tensor graph Multi-GPU (single node) Single process, automatic sharding One process per GPU (torchrun) Ecosystem Flax, Optax, Equinox (modular) torchvision, torchaudio, HuggingFace (large) Best for Custom research, scientific computing, novel architectures Production ML, large model ecosystem, community support

Tip

If you are starting a new project and your workflow is standard (image classification, NLP fine-tuning, etc.), PyTorch and its ecosystem will get you running faster. If you need custom differentiation, higher-order gradients, or XLA-level performance tuning, JAX is a strong choice.

docs.title: Julia and SciML
docs.text: Julia is well suited for scientific machine learning (SciML), combining the expressiveness of a high-level language with the performance of compiled code. AICR provides Julia through the module system with GPU support via CUDA.jl.

docs.title: Loading Julia
docs.text:
Load Julia via the module system:

$ module spider julia
$ module load julia
$ julia --version

docs.title: Package Management with Pkg.jl
docs.text: Julia uses Pkg.jl for dependency management. Every project should have its own environment defined by Project.toml and Manifest.toml files.

docs.title: Setting JULIA_DEPOT_PATH
docs.text:
Julia stores downloaded packages, compiled artifacts, and registries in its depot directory (defaults to ~/.julia). This can quickly consume your 100 GB home quota. Redirect it to scratch:

$ echo 'export JULIA_DEPOT_PATH=/scratch/$USER/.julia' >> ~/.bashrc
$ source ~/.bashrc

Warning

Set JULIA_DEPOT_PATH before installing any packages. Moving an existing depot can cause path mismatches in compiled artifacts.

docs.title: Creating a Project Environment
docs.text:
$ mkdir -p /scratch/$USER/my_project
$ cd /scratch/$USER/my_project
$ julia --project=.

Inside the Julia REPL:

using Pkg
Pkg.add("DifferentialEquations")
Pkg.add("Lux")
Pkg.add("CUDA")

This creates Project.toml and Manifest.toml files in the project directory. Always commit these files to version control for reproducibility.

docs.title: Activating an Existing Environment
docs.text:
$ julia --project=/scratch/$USER/my_project

Or from the Julia REPL:

using Pkg
Pkg.activate("/scratch/$USER/my_project")
Pkg.instantiate()  # install all packages from Manifest.toml

docs.title: The SciML Ecosystem
docs.text:
The SciML ecosystem is a collection of Julia packages for scientific computing and machine learning. Key packages include:

Package Purpose DifferentialEquations.jl Solvers for ODEs, SDEs, PDEs, DAEs ModelingToolkit.jl Symbolic modeling and automatic equation simplification Lux.jl Neural network framework (SciML-native) Optimization.jl Unified optimization interface SciMLSensitivity.jl Adjoint methods for differentiating through solvers NeuralPDE.jl Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs)

docs.title: DifferentialEquations.jl
docs.text:
DifferentialEquations.jl provides high-performance solvers for a wide range of differential equations:

using DifferentialEquations

function lorenz!(du, u, p, t)
    σ, ρ, β = p
    du[1] = σ * (u[2] - u[1])
    du[2] = u[1] * (ρ - u[3]) - u[2]
    du[3] = u[1] * u[2] - β * u[3]
end

u0 = [1.0, 0.0, 0.0]
tspan = (0.0, 100.0)
p = [10.0, 28.0, 8/3]

prob = ODEProblem(lorenz!, u0, tspan, p)
sol = solve(prob, Tsit5())

docs.title: ModelingToolkit.jl
docs.text:
ModelingToolkit.jl allows you to define models symbolically and let the compiler optimize the resulting system:

using ModelingToolkit
using ModelingToolkit: t_nounits as t, D_nounits as D

@variables x(t) y(t) z(t)
@parameters σ ρ β

eqs = [
    D(x) ~ σ * (y - x),
    D(y) ~ x * (ρ - z) - y,
    D(z) ~ x * y - β * z,
]

@named lorenz = ODESystem(eqs, t)
lorenz = structural_simplify(lorenz)

docs.title: Neural ODEs with Lux.jl
docs.text:
Lux.jl is the SciML ecosystem's neural network framework, designed for scientific machine learning with explicit parameter handling.

A neural ODE combines a neural network with a differential equation solver:

using Lux, DifferentialEquations, SciMLSensitivity
using Zygote, Optimisers, Random

# Define the neural network
model = Chain(
    Dense(2 => 32, tanh),
    Dense(32 => 32, tanh),
    Dense(32 => 2),
)

rng = Random.default_rng()
ps, st = Lux.setup(rng, model)

# Neural ODE dynamics
function neural_ode!(du, u, p, t)
    du .= first(model(u, p, st))
end

# Solve and train
prob = ODEProblem(neural_ode!, u0, tspan, ps)

function loss(p)
    pred = solve(prob, Tsit5(); p=p, saveat=0.1)
    sum(abs2, pred .- target_data)
end

docs.title: GPU Computing with CUDA.jl
docs.text:
CUDA.jl provides GPU acceleration for Julia. Load the CUDA module alongside Julia:

$ module load cuda julia

Verify GPU access in Julia:

using CUDA
CUDA.functional()    # should return true
CUDA.devices()       # list available GPUs
CUDA.versioninfo()   # CUDA toolkit and driver info

Move arrays to the GPU with CuArray:

using CUDA

a = CUDA.randn(1000, 1000)
b = CUDA.randn(1000, 1000)
c = a * b  # runs on GPU

docs.title: GPU-Accelerated Differential Equations
docs.text:
DifferentialEquations.jl can solve ensembles of problems on the GPU:

using DifferentialEquations, CUDA

prob = ODEProblem(lorenz!, u0, tspan, p)
ensemble_prob = EnsembleProblem(prob)

sol = solve(
    ensemble_prob, Tsit5(),
    EnsembleGPUKernel(CUDA.CUDABackend()),
    trajectories = 10_000,
)

Note

GPU-accelerated ensemble solves are most effective when you need to solve many independent instances of the same problem (e.g., parameter sweeps, uncertainty quantification).

docs.title: Batch Job Script
docs.text:
Submit a Julia SciML job on a GPU partition:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=julia_sciml
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=32G
#SBATCH --time=08:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load cuda julia

export JULIA_DEPOT_PATH=/scratch/$USER/.julia

julia --project=/scratch/$USER/my_project train.jl

Submit with:

$ sbatch julia_sciml.sh

Tip

Julia has a noticeable compilation delay the first time code runs ("time to first plot"). For production jobs, consider using PackageCompiler.jl to create a custom system image with your dependencies precompiled.

docs.title: Multi-Threading
docs.text:
Julia supports multi-threading natively. Set the number of threads to match your Slurm allocation:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=julia_threaded
#SBATCH --partition=cpu
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=16
#SBATCH --mem=32G
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load julia

export JULIA_DEPOT_PATH=/scratch/$USER/.julia
export JULIA_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK

julia --project=/scratch/$USER/my_project --threads=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK run.jl

Inside your Julia code, use Threads.@threads for parallel loops:

using Base.Threads

results = zeros(1000)
@threads for i in 1:1000
    results[i] = expensive_computation(i)
end

println("Using $(nthreads()) threads")

Warning

Do not set JULIA_NUM_THREADS higher than --cpus-per-task. Oversubscribing CPU cores causes contention and slows your job.

docs.title: Working with Large Datasets
docs.text: Training on large datasets requires careful data staging, efficient I/O, and the right storage tier. AICR's all-flash VAST storage with NFS-RDMA provides high throughput, but how you organize and load your data still matters.

docs.title: Storage Tiers for Large Data
docs.text:
Storage Path Quota Best For Apps /apps/datasets/, /apps/models/ Shared Cluster-wide shared datasets and pre-trained models (read-only for users) Scratch /scratch/$USER/ 10 TB Active training data, checkpoints, temporary files Work /work/INSTITUTION/ Varies Institution-level shared datasets, curated data, collaboration Home /home/$USER/ 100 GB Code and configs only — too small for datasets
  • Apps (/apps) is a cluster-wide shared directory on VAST storage. Pre-staged datasets and models are available here for all users. Check /apps/datasets/ and /apps/models/ before downloading your own copy.
  • Scratch is where your active training data should live. It is fast, large, and designed for job I/O. Scratch is subject to regular purging.
  • Work is for shared, longer-term project data. Store curated datasets here so teammates can access them without duplicating copies in individual scratch directories.

Warning

Do not store large datasets in your home directory. The 100 GB quota fills quickly and the home filesystem is not designed for high-throughput data loading.

docs.title: Data Staging Workflow
docs.text:
The recommended workflow for large datasets follows a stage-compute-archive pattern:

Transfer --> Scratch --> Compute --> Results --> Work

docs.title: 1. Transfer Data to Scratch
docs.text:
Move your dataset onto the cluster. For large transfers, use Globus or rsync:

$ rsync -avP /local/path/to/dataset/ USERNAME@transfer.aicr.ai:/scratch/$USER/datasets/my_dataset/

For datasets from remote repositories:

$ cd /scratch/$USER/datasets
$ wget https://example.com/dataset.tar.gz
$ tar -xzf dataset.tar.gz
$ rm dataset.tar.gz

docs.title: 2. Compute from Scratch
docs.text:
Point your training scripts at the scratch path:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=train_large
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=4
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=32
#SBATCH --mem=128G
#SBATCH --time=24:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load cuda

eval "$(conda shell.bash hook)"
conda activate myenv

python train.py --data-dir /scratch/$USER/datasets/my_dataset

docs.title: 3. Archive Results to Work
docs.text:
After training completes, copy important outputs to work for longer-term storage:

$ cp -r /scratch/$USER/results/experiment_01 /work/INSTITUTION/PROJECT/results/

Tip

Scratch is purged regularly. Do not rely on it for long-term storage. Move final models, metrics, and key artifacts to /work/INSTITUTION/ or transfer them off-cluster.

docs.title: NFS-RDMA Performance
docs.text:
AICR uses VAST all-flash storage with NFS-RDMA, providing high-bandwidth access from every compute node. To get the best performance:

  • Use multiple parallel connections -- the storage system scales with concurrent I/O. Using 16 parallel data loading workers can saturate the available bandwidth far better than a single-threaded reader.
  • Read large sequential blocks -- formats that store data contiguously (binary files, memory-mapped arrays, sharded archives) outperform millions of tiny individual files.
  • Avoid many small files -- directories with hundreds of thousands of small files (e.g., one PNG per sample) create metadata bottlenecks. Use sharded formats instead.

Note

With 16 parallel connections per node, the NFS-RDMA fabric delivers high aggregate throughput. Structure your data pipeline to take advantage of this concurrency.

docs.title: PyTorch DataLoader Optimization
docs.text: The PyTorch DataLoader is the primary interface for feeding data to GPU training. Tuning its parameters can dramatically improve throughput.

docs.title: Key Parameters
docs.text:
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

train_loader = DataLoader(
    dataset,
    batch_size=64,
    shuffle=True,
    num_workers=8,         # parallel data loading processes
    pin_memory=True,       # use pinned (page-locked) memory for faster GPU transfer
    prefetch_factor=2,     # batches prefetched per worker
    persistent_workers=True,  # keep workers alive between epochs
)
Parameter Recommended Value Why num_workers 4--8 per GPU Parallel data loading saturates I/O; match to --cpus-per-task pin_memory True Enables async CPU-to-GPU memory transfer prefetch_factor 2--4 Prefetches batches so the GPU never waits on data persistent_workers True Avoids worker respawn overhead between epochs

Tip

A good starting point is num_workers = 4 * NUM_GPUS. If you have --cpus-per-task=32 and 4 GPUs, set num_workers=8 per DataLoader. Monitor GPU utilization with nvidia-smi -- if GPUs are idle between steps, increase num_workers or prefetch_factor.

docs.title: Example Batch Job
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=train_dataloader
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=4
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=32
#SBATCH --mem=128G
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load cuda

eval "$(conda shell.bash hook)"
conda activate myenv

python train.py \
    --data-dir /scratch/$USER/datasets/imagenet \
    --num-workers 8 \
    --batch-size 64

docs.title: HuggingFace Streaming Mode
docs.text:
For datasets too large to download entirely, or when you want to start training immediately, use HuggingFace's streaming mode:

from datasets import load_dataset

dataset = load_dataset(
    "allenai/c4",
    "en",
    split="train",
    streaming=True,
)

for example in dataset:
    # Process examples one at a time -- no full download needed
    tokens = tokenizer(example["text"])

Streaming mode:

  • Downloads data on the fly, so you can begin training immediately
  • Uses minimal disk space -- no need to store the full dataset
  • Works with IterableDataset and PyTorch DataLoader
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

torch_dataset = dataset.with_format("torch")
loader = DataLoader(torch_dataset, batch_size=32)

Note

Streaming requires network access from compute nodes. AICR compute nodes have outbound internet, so streaming works directly from jobs.

docs.title: Sharded Dataset Formats
docs.text: For very large datasets (hundreds of GB to TB scale), sharded formats are more efficient than directories of individual files. They reduce metadata overhead and enable parallel reading.

docs.title: WebDataset
docs.text:
WebDataset stores data in tar archives (shards) that can be read sequentially at high speed:

import os
import webdataset as wds

shard_path = os.path.expandvars("/scratch/$USER/datasets/imagenet-shards/shard-{000000..001000}.tar")
dataset = (
    wds.WebDataset(shard_path)
    .decode("pil")
    .to_tuple("jpg", "cls")
    .batched(64)
)

loader = wds.WebLoader(dataset, num_workers=8, batch_size=None)

docs.title: Mosaic StreamingDataset
docs.text:
Mosaic StreamingDataset writes data in an optimized binary format and supports deterministic, resumable streaming:

import os
from streaming import StreamingDataset

dataset = StreamingDataset(
    local=os.path.expandvars("/scratch/$USER/datasets/my_mosaic_dataset"),
    shuffle=True,
)

docs.title: Creating Sharded Datasets
docs.text:
Convert your dataset to shards before training, not during:

$ python create_shards.py \
    --input /scratch/$USER/datasets/raw_images/ \
    --output /scratch/$USER/datasets/shards/ \
    --shard-size 1GB

Tip

Shard sizes of 256 MB to 1 GB generally work well. Larger shards reduce metadata overhead; smaller shards improve shuffle quality and enable more parallelism.

docs.title: Shared Dataset Repository
docs.text:
If multiple users in your project need the same dataset, store a single copy in the shared work directory rather than duplicating it across individual scratch directories:

/work/INSTITUTION/datasets/
    imagenet/
    c4/
    openwebtext/

Set appropriate group permissions so all project members can read:

$ chmod -R g+rX /work/INSTITUTION/datasets/my_dataset

Note

Coordinate with your team to decide who prepares and maintains shared datasets. A single curated copy in /work/INSTITUTION/datasets/ saves storage and avoids version inconsistencies.

docs.title: AI Tools and Workflows
docs.text:
AICR is built for AI research. With 248 NVIDIA B200 GPUs and 152 RTX PRO 6000 GPUs connected by NDR400 InfiniBand, it is designed for training, fine-tuning, and running inference on modern AI models — from small experiments on a single GPU to distributed training across multiple nodes.

AI on AICR is broadly defined. The cluster serves researchers across all disciplines who apply AI methods: machine learning, deep learning, NLP, computer vision, scientific ML, drug discovery, climate modeling, digital humanities, computational social science, and more.

docs.text:
• Frameworks: PyTorch, JAX, and TensorFlow are available via modules, Conda, or containers
  • Model Ecosystem: Hugging Face Transformers, Datasets, and Accelerate
  • Inference: vLLM for multi-GPU LLM serving
  • Containers: Apptainer for packaging complex environments with GPU support
  • Experiment Tracking: Weights & Biases, MLflow, TensorBoard
  • Interconnect: NDR400 InfiniBand (400 Gb/s) enables fast distributed training with NCCL

docs.title: PyTorch on AICR
docs.text: PyTorch is the most widely used deep learning framework on AICR. This page covers installation, GPU verification, single-GPU and multi-GPU training, and performance tips. For scaling strategies beyond a single node, see Distributed Training.

docs.title: Installing PyTorch
docs.text:
pipCondaContainer 
$ python3 -m venv ~/pytorch-env
$ source ~/pytorch-env/bin/activate
$ pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu124

$ conda create --prefix /scratch/$USER/conda-envs/pytorch python=3.12
$ conda activate /scratch/$USER/conda-envs/pytorch
$ conda install pytorch torchvision torchaudio pytorch-cuda=12.4 -c pytorch -c nvidia

Pull the NVIDIA PyTorch container, which includes PyTorch, CUDA, cuDNN, and NCCL pre-configured:

$ apptainer pull /scratch/$USER/containers/pytorch_24.12.sif docker://nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:24.12-py3
$ apptainer exec --nv /scratch/$USER/containers/pytorch_24.12.sif python train.py

See Containers (Apptainer) for building custom containers.

Large environments

ML environments can exceed 10 GB. Create them on scratch to avoid filling your 100 GB home quota. See Python and Conda for details.

docs.title: Verifying GPU Access
docs.text:
After installing PyTorch, confirm that it can see the GPUs allocated to your job:

import torch
print(torch.cuda.is_available())       # True
print(torch.cuda.device_count())       # number of GPUs in your job
print(torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))   # GPU model name

Expected output on a B200 node:

True
8
NVIDIA B200

docs.title: Single-GPU Training
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=pytorch_single
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=32G
#SBATCH --time=08:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
source ~/pytorch-env/bin/activate

python train.py --epochs 50 --batch-size 64

docs.title: Multi-GPU Training (Single Node)
docs.text:
Use torchrun to launch one process per GPU with PyTorch Distributed Data Parallel (DDP). On AICR, each node has 8 GPUs:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=pytorch_multigpu
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gpus-per-node=8
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=64
#SBATCH --mem=0
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
source ~/pytorch-env/bin/activate

torchrun --nproc_per_node=8 train.py

Note

Setting --mem=0 requests all available memory on the node, which is typical for exclusive multi-GPU jobs.

Your training script must initialize the process group and wrap the model in DistributedDataParallel. See the train.py example in the aicr-doc-boot repository, or the PyTorch Training recipe for a complete walkthrough.

docs.title: Multi-Node Training
docs.text:
For training across multiple nodes, combine srun with torchrun. This launches torchrun on each node via Slurm, and the processes coordinate through NCCL over InfiniBand:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=pytorch_multinode
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --nodes=2
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=1
#SBATCH --gpus-per-node=8
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=64
#SBATCH --mem=0
#SBATCH --time=24:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
source ~/pytorch-env/bin/activate

export MASTER_ADDR=$(scontrol show hostnames $SLURM_JOB_NODELIST | head -n 1)
export MASTER_PORT=29500

srun torchrun \
    --nnodes=$SLURM_NNODES \
    --nproc_per_node=8 \
    --rdzv_id=$SLURM_JOB_ID \
    --rdzv_backend=c10d \
    --rdzv_endpoint=$MASTER_ADDR:$MASTER_PORT \
    train.py

For choosing the right parallelism strategy (DDP, FSDP, pipeline), see Distributed Training. For NCCL tuning and InfiniBand details, see Multi-GPU Computing.

docs.title: Data Loading
docs.text:
Use pin_memory and multiple workers in your DataLoader to overlap data transfer with computation:

train_loader = torch.utils.data.DataLoader(
    dataset,
    batch_size=64,
    num_workers=8,
    pin_memory=True,
    persistent_workers=True,
    prefetch_factor=2,
)

Tip

A good starting point is 4 workers per GPU. Monitor CPU utilization; if all workers are saturated, increase --cpus-per-task in your Slurm script.

docs.title: Mixed Precision Training
docs.text:
Use torch.amp to train in mixed precision (FP16/BF16), which reduces memory usage and speeds up training on both RTX PRO 6000 and B200 GPUs:

scaler = torch.amp.GradScaler()

for batch in train_loader:
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    with torch.amp.autocast(device_type="cuda", dtype=torch.bfloat16):
        output = model(batch)
        loss = criterion(output, targets)
    scaler.scale(loss).backward()
    scaler.step(optimizer)
    scaler.update()

Note

B200 GPUs natively support BF16 and FP8. Use dtype=torch.bfloat16 for best results on B200 hardware.

docs.title: Compilation
docs.text:
torch.compile can significantly speed up training and inference by fusing operations:

model = torch.compile(model)

docs.title: Batch Size and Gradient Accumulation
docs.text:
Increase batch size to better utilize GPU memory. If you run out of memory, use gradient accumulation instead of reducing batch size:

accumulation_steps = 4

for i, batch in enumerate(train_loader):
    with torch.amp.autocast(device_type="cuda"):
        loss = model(batch) / accumulation_steps
    loss.backward()
    if (i + 1) % accumulation_steps == 0:
        optimizer.step()
        optimizer.zero_grad()

docs.title: Gradient Checkpointing
docs.text:
If your model runs out of GPU memory, enable gradient checkpointing to trade compute for memory:

from torch.utils.checkpoint import checkpoint_sequential

output = checkpoint_sequential(model.layers, segments=4, input=x)

docs.title: Domain Libraries
docs.text:
Many domain-specific libraries build on PyTorch:

Library Domain Install torchvision Computer vision pip install torchvision torchaudio Audio processing pip install torchaudio PyTorch Geometric Graph neural networks pip install torch_geometric ESMFold / OpenFold Protein structure prediction See Protein Folding recipe torchtext NLP preprocessing pip install torchtext Lightning High-level training framework pip install lightning

These can be installed via pip or conda in your environment.

docs.title: R for ML
docs.text: R is well suited for statistical modeling, data analysis, and increasingly for deep learning. AICR provides R through the module system, with GPU support for packages like torch for R.

docs.title: Loading R
docs.text:
Load R via the module system:

$ module spider R
$ module load R
$ R --version

Tip

Start every job script with module purge followed by explicit module load commands. This ensures reproducible environments.

docs.title: Installing R Packages
docs.text:
R packages install to your home directory by default. For large package libraries, set a library path on scratch to avoid filling your 100 GB home quota:

$ mkdir -p /scratch/$USER/R/library
$ echo 'R_LIBS_USER=/scratch/$USER/R/library' >> ~/.Renviron

Then install packages from within R:

install.packages("tidymodels")

docs.title: The tidymodels Ecosystem
docs.text:
tidymodels provides a consistent interface for machine learning in R, covering data splitting, preprocessing, model fitting, tuning, and evaluation.

Key packages in the ecosystem:

Package Purpose rsample Data splitting and resampling recipes Feature engineering and preprocessing parsnip Unified model interface tune Hyperparameter tuning yardstick Model evaluation metrics workflows Combine preprocessing and modeling steps

Install the full ecosystem:

install.packages("tidymodels")

A typical tidymodels workflow:

library(tidymodels)

# Split data
split <- initial_split(my_data, prop = 0.8)
train <- training(split)
test  <- testing(split)

# Define preprocessing
rec <- recipe(target ~ ., data = train) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors())

# Define model
model <- rand_forest(trees = 500, mtry = tune(), min_n = tune()) |>
  set_engine("ranger") |>
  set_mode("classification")

# Create workflow
wf <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(rec) |>
  add_model(model)

# Tune hyperparameters
results <- tune_grid(wf, resamples = vfold_cv(train, v = 5), grid = 20)

docs.title: torch for R (GPU Deep Learning)
docs.text:
The torch package provides GPU-accelerated deep learning directly in R, without requiring a Python installation. It uses the same underlying C++ LibTorch library as PyTorch.

Install torch with CUDA support:

install.packages("torch")
library(torch)
torch::torch_is_installed()
torch::cuda_is_available()

Note

The torch R package downloads its own LibTorch binaries. Ensure CUDA is loaded via module load cuda before installing so that torch detects GPU support.

A minimal neural network example:

library(torch)

net <- nn_module(
  initialize = function(in_features, hidden, out_features) {
    self$fc1 <- nn_linear(in_features, hidden)
    self$fc2 <- nn_linear(hidden, out_features)
  },
  forward = function(x) {
    x |>
      self$fc1() |>
      nnf_relu() |>
      self$fc2()
  }
)

model <- net(in_features = 10, hidden = 64, out_features = 1)
model$to(device = "cuda")

docs.title: RStudio via OnDemand
docs.text:
If RStudio is available through the OnDemand web portal, you can launch interactive R sessions on compute nodes with GPU access.

  1. Log in to the OnDemand portal
  2. Select Interactive Apps > RStudio (if available)
  3. Choose a partition, number of GPUs, memory, and time
  4. Click Launch and wait for the session to start
  5. Click Connect to open RStudio in your browser

Alternatively, run RStudio Server from a job and connect via SSH tunnel:

$ ssh -L 8787:NODE_NAME:8787 USERNAME@login.aicr.ai

Then open http://localhost:8787 in your browser.

docs.title: Batch R Job on rtx-batch
docs.text:
Submit an R script as a batch job:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=r_torch
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=32G
#SBATCH --time=08:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load cuda R

Rscript train_model.R

Submit with:

$ sbatch r_torch.sh

Tip

Use --cpus-per-task=8 or more when using torch for R. Data loading and preprocessing benefit from multiple CPU cores alongside the GPU.

docs.title: Parallel R with future and furrr
docs.text:
The future and furrr packages provide a portable framework for parallel R. They work well for embarrassingly parallel tasks like cross-validation, bootstrap resampling, and batch predictions.

Install the packages:

install.packages(c("future", "furrr"))

Use plan(multisession) to run across CPU cores:

library(future)
library(furrr)

plan(multisession, workers = availableCores())

# Parallel map — runs each iteration on a separate core
results <- future_map(1:100, function(i) {
  # Fit model with different random seed
  set.seed(i)
  fit <- train_my_model(data)
  fit$metrics
})

docs.title: Batch Job for Parallel R on cpu
docs.text:
For CPU-intensive parallel R workflows, use the cpu partition:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=r_parallel
#SBATCH --partition=cpu
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=32
#SBATCH --mem=64G
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load R

Rscript parallel_analysis.R

Inside parallel_analysis.R:

library(future)
library(furrr)
library(tidymodels)

# Use all allocated cores
plan(multisession, workers = parallelly::availableCores())

# Parallel cross-validation with tidymodels
results <- tune_grid(
  wf,
  resamples = vfold_cv(train_data, v = 10, repeats = 5),
  grid = 30,
  control = control_grid(parallel_over = "everything")
)

Warning

Match the workers count to your --cpus-per-task allocation. Spawning more workers than allocated cores causes contention and slows your job. Use parallelly::availableCores() to automatically detect the Slurm allocation.
