docs.text:
• Filesystem Overview — quotas, snapshots, and purge policies
  • Transferring Data — Globus, scp, rsync
  • Large Datasets — data staging and I/O optimization
  • Networking — how storage connects to the cluster

docs.title: Analysis and Data Processing
docs.text: The cpu partition provides high-memory nodes for data analysis, preprocessing, and workflow orchestration. These nodes do not have GPUs — use them for work that benefits from many CPU cores and large memory rather than GPU acceleration.

docs.title: CPU Partition Specs
docs.text: Property Value Nodes w-series (5 nodes) Cores per node 128 Memory per node 1 TB GPUs None Max wall time 24 hours Default time 15 minutes

docs.title: When to Use the CPU Partition
docs.text:
• Data preprocessing: cleaning, transforming, or merging large datasets before GPU training
  • Parallel analysis: running analysis in parallel across many cores with Dask, mpi4py, or GNU Parallel
  • Post-processing: aggregating results, generating reports, statistical analysis
  • Workflow orchestration: coordinating multi-step pipelines that launch GPU jobs

For GPU-accelerated work (training, inference, anything using CUDA), use the rtx-* or b200-* partitions instead.

docs.title: Example: Basic CPU Job
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=data_analysis
#SBATCH --partition=cpu
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=32
#SBATCH --mem=128G
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load python

python analyze_data.py

docs.title: Parallel Analysis with Dask
docs.text: Dask scales Python data analysis (Pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn) across multiple cores or nodes. It is well-suited for the CPU partition's large-memory nodes.

docs.title: Single-Node Dask
docs.text:
For analysis that fits on one node but benefits from parallel processing:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=dask_analysis
#SBATCH --partition=cpu
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=64
#SBATCH --mem=512G
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load python

python dask_analysis.py

In your Python script:

import dask.dataframe as dd
from dask.distributed import Client

# Use local cluster with allocated CPUs
client = Client(n_workers=16, threads_per_worker=4)

# Read and process a large dataset in parallel
df = dd.read_parquet("/scratch/USERNAME/data/large_dataset.parquet")
result = df.groupby("category").agg({"value": "mean"}).compute()
result.to_csv("/scratch/USERNAME/results/summary.csv")

docs.title: Multi-Node Dask
docs.text: For datasets that exceed a single node's memory or processing capacity, see the Parallel Data Analysis with Dask recipe.

docs.title: Parallel Analysis with mpi4py
docs.text:
For MPI-based parallel analysis across CPU nodes:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=mpi_analysis
#SBATCH --partition=cpu
#SBATCH --nodes=2
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=16
#SBATCH --time=02:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load python

srun --mpi=pmix python parallel_analysis.py

Note

AICR uses PMIx for MPI process management. Always pass --mpi=pmix to srun.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Slurm Basics — general job submission
  • Parallel Data Analysis with Dask — detailed Dask recipe
  • Working with Large Datasets — staging and I/O patterns

docs.title: GPU Jobs
docs.text: AICR's primary resource is its GPUs. This page covers how to request GPUs, choose the right partition, and submit GPU jobs. For GPU hardware details, see GPU Overview.

docs.title: Choosing a Partition
docs.text:
Partition GPU Model GPUs/Node Max Time Best For rtx-batch RTX PRO 6000 8 24h Production GPU workloads rtx-devel RTX PRO 6000 8 4h Testing and debugging b200-batch B200 8 24h Large-scale AI/ML training b200-devel B200 8 4h Testing and debugging

Tip

Use devel partitions for testing — they have shorter queue times. Switch to batch for production runs.

docs.title: Requesting GPUs
docs.text:
The simplest way to request GPUs:

#SBATCH --gpus=1                    # one GPU (any type on the partition)
#SBATCH --gpus=4                    # four GPUs
#SBATCH --gpus-per-node=8           # all 8 GPUs on a node

To request a specific GPU type, use GRES syntax:

#SBATCH --gres=gpu:rtx6000:4    # 4 RTX PRO 6000 GPUs
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:b200:8       # 8 B200 GPUs

Note

Match your partition to your GPU type. Use rtx-batch/rtx-devel for RTX GPUs and b200-batch/b200-devel for B200 GPUs.

docs.title: Single-GPU Job
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=single_gpu
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=32G
#SBATCH --time=08:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load cuda

python train.py

docs.title: Multi-GPU Job (Single Node)
docs.text:
Request multiple GPUs on one node:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=multi_gpu
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gpus-per-node=4
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=32
#SBATCH --mem=128G
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

module purge
module load cuda

torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 train.py

For multi-node GPU jobs (distributed training across nodes), see Distributed Training and Multi-GPU Computing.

docs.title: Verifying GPU Access
docs.text:
Inside a running job, check that GPUs are visible:

$ nvidia-smi

This shows GPU model, memory, utilization, and temperature. If no GPUs appear, verify that you requested them with --gpus or --gres and submitted to a GPU partition.

For PyTorch:

import torch
print(torch.cuda.is_available())       # should print True
print(torch.cuda.device_count())       # should match requested GPUs
print(torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))   # GPU model name

docs.title: GPU Memory Considerations
docs.text:
• RTX PRO 6000 and B200 GPUs each have substantial memory, but large models or large batch sizes can still cause out-of-memory (OOM) errors
  • If you encounter OOM: reduce batch size, enable mixed precision (torch.amp), or use gradient checkpointing
  • Monitor GPU memory usage with nvidia-smi during interactive sessions to right-size your requests

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• GPU Overview — hardware specs and architecture
  • Multi-GPU Computing — NCCL and GPU-to-GPU communication
  • Distributed Training — scaling beyond one node
  • Slurm Basics — general job submission

docs.title: Job Arrays
docs.text: Job arrays let you submit many similar jobs from a single script. Each task gets a unique index, so you can vary parameters, process different input files, or run repeated experiments — all with one sbatch command.

docs.title: Basic Syntax
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=sweep
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4
#SBATCH --mem=16G
#SBATCH --time=02:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --array=0-49
#SBATCH --output=%x-%a-%j.out

module purge
module load cuda python

python train.py --seed=$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID

Submit once, and Slurm creates 50 independent jobs (task IDs 0 through 49). Each reads $SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID to vary its behavior.

$ sbatch sweep.sh
Submitted batch job 12345

docs.title: Limiting Concurrency
docs.text:
To avoid monopolizing resources, limit how many tasks run at once:

#SBATCH --array=0-99%10    # run at most 10 tasks simultaneously

Tip

Limiting concurrency is good practice — it leaves resources available for other users and helps your fairshare score.

docs.title: Varying Random Seeds
docs.text: python train.py --seed=$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID

docs.title: Processing Multiple Files
docs.text:
FILES=(/scratch/$USER/data/input_*.csv)
python process.py --input=${FILES[$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID]}

docs.title: Hyperparameter Sweep
docs.text:
LEARNING_RATES=(0.001 0.0005 0.0001 0.00005 0.00001)
python train.py --lr=${LEARNING_RATES[$SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID]}

For more sophisticated sweeps with experiment tracking, see Hyperparameter Sweeps.

docs.title: Output File Naming
docs.text:
Use %a in output filenames to include the array task ID:

#SBATCH --output=%x-%a-%j.out    # jobname-taskid-jobid.out

Each task writes to a separate file, so outputs don't mix.

docs.title: Array Limits
docs.text:
• Maximum array size: 10,000 tasks
  • Array indices can be non-contiguous: --array=1,3,5,7 or --array=0-100:10 (step by 10)

docs.title: Managing Array Jobs
docs.text:
Cancel all tasks in an array:

$ scancel JOBID

Cancel a specific task:

$ scancel JOBID_TASKID    # e.g., scancel 12345_42

Check status of all tasks:

$ squeue -j JOBID

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Slurm Basics — general job submission
  • Monitoring Jobs — tracking job status
  • Hyperparameter Sweeps — combining arrays with experiment tracking
  • Experiment Tracking — W&B and MLflow

docs.title: Check Your Jobs
docs.text:
View your running and pending jobs:

$ squeue -u $USER

View jobs on a specific partition:

$ squeue -p rtx-batch

docs.title: Detailed Job Information
docs.text:
Inspect a running or pending job:

$ scontrol show job JOBID

This shows the full job configuration: allocated nodes, resources, time limits, working directory, and more.

docs.title: Job History and Resource Usage
docs.text:
After a job completes, use sacct to see what resources it actually used:

$ sacct -j JOBID --format=JobID,JobName,Partition,Elapsed,MaxRSS,State

Check your recent job history:

$ sacct -u $USER --starttime=now-7days --format=JobID,JobName,Partition,Elapsed,State,ExitCode

Tip

Use sacct to check whether your jobs are using the resources you requested. If MaxRSS is much lower than your --mem request, reduce memory in future jobs to improve your fairshare.

docs.title: Useful sacct Format Fields
docs.text: Field Description JobID Job identifier JobName Job name Partition Partition used Elapsed Actual wall time MaxRSS Peak memory usage State Final job state (COMPLETED, FAILED, TIMEOUT, etc.) ExitCode Exit code (0 = success) AllocCPUS CPUs allocated AllocTRES All resources allocated (CPUs, memory, GPUs)

docs.title: Partition and Node Status
docs.text:
$ sinfo

See which nodes are available, allocated, or down. Useful for checking whether your target partition has capacity.

$ sinfo -p rtx-batch --format="%N %T %G %m"

This shows node names, state, GPUs, and memory for a specific partition.

docs.title: Cancel a Job
docs.text:
$ scancel JOBID

Cancel all your jobs:

$ scancel -u $USER

Cancel a specific array task:

$ scancel JOBID_TASKID

docs.title: Checking GPU Utilization in a Running Job
docs.text:
If you have a running GPU job, check GPU utilization by connecting to the node:

$ squeue -u $USER                    # find your node name
$ ssh NODE_NAME nvidia-smi           # check GPU usage (requires active job on that node)

Note

You can only SSH to compute nodes where you have an active Slurm job.

docs.title: Jobstats
docs.text: Jobstats is available for reviewing job performance — CPU, memory, and GPU utilization for completed and in-progress jobs. This is useful for identifying underutilized resources and right-sizing future requests.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Slurm Basics — submitting jobs
  • Job Troubleshooting — diagnosing failures
  • Efficient Resource Usage — right-sizing requests

docs.title: Slurm Basics
docs.text:
AICR uses Slurm (version 25.11.1) to manage compute resources and schedule jobs. You request resources, Slurm finds available nodes, and your job runs there.

Danger

Do not run computation on login nodes. They are limited to 4 CPU cores and 8 GB memory per user. Processes exceeding these limits will be terminated.

docs.title: Partitions
docs.text:
Partition GPUs/Node Max Time Default Time Use Case cpu — 24h 15 min Data analysis, workflow orchestration rtx-batch 8 24h 1h RTX GPU batch jobs rtx-devel 8 4h 15 min RTX GPU development and testing b200-batch 8 24h 1h B200 GPU batch jobs b200-devel 8 4h 15 min B200 GPU development and testing

Default memory: 1 GB per CPU. Devel partitions limited to 4 concurrent jobs per user. For GPU hardware details, see GPU Overview.

$ sinfo

docs.title: Submitting a Batch Job
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=my_analysis       # name for identification
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch        # which partition
#SBATCH --nodes=1                    # number of nodes
#SBATCH --ntasks=1                   # number of tasks (processes)
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=4            # CPU cores per task
#SBATCH --gpus=1                     # number of GPUs
#SBATCH --mem=16G                    # memory
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00              # max wall time (HH:MM:SS)
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME       # institution account (required)
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out           # stdout file

module purge
module load cuda

python train.py

$ sbatch my_job.sh
Submitted batch job 12345

Tip

Always specify --account. Valid accounts: bu, hu, mit, neu, umass, uml, umb, umassd, umassmed, yale, ma, aihub.

docs.title: Interactive Sessions
docs.text:
$ salloc --partition=rtx-devel --gpus=1 --cpus-per-task=4 --mem=16G --time=01:00:00 --account=ACCOUNT_NAME

This gives you a shell on a compute node. Type exit when done.

docs.title: Output Files
docs.text: Pattern Expands To %j Job ID %x Job name %a Array task ID %N First node hostname

docs.title: Common Directives
docs.text: Directive Description Example --job-name Job name --job-name=training --partition Partition --partition=b200-batch --account Institution account --account=mit --nodes Node count --nodes=2 --ntasks Total tasks --ntasks=8 --ntasks-per-node Tasks per node --ntasks-per-node=4 --cpus-per-task CPUs per task --cpus-per-task=8 --mem Memory per node --mem=64G --gpus Total GPUs --gpus=2 --gpus-per-node GPUs per node --gpus-per-node=8 --gres Generic resource --gres=gpu:2 --time Wall time --time=12:00:00 --output Stdout file --output=%x-%j.out --error Stderr file --error=%x-%j.err --array Job array --array=0-99%10 --exclusive Exclusive node --exclusive --dependency Wait for job --dependency=afterok:12345 --mail-type Email alerts --mail-type=END,FAIL

docs.title: Resource Defaults
docs.text: Setting Value Default memory per CPU 1 GB Batch max wall time 24 hours Devel max wall time 4 hours Max concurrent devel jobs 4 per user Max job array size 10,000 tasks

docs.title: Fairshare
docs.text: AICR allocates resources fairly using a three-level hierarchy: Institution → Project → User. All institutions have equal shares. Your priority depends on recent usage relative to your institution's and project's allocation. Specify --account correctly and Slurm handles the rest.

docs.title: Slurm Environment Variables
docs.text:
Inside a running job:

Variable Contents $SLURM_JOB_ID Job ID $SLURM_JOB_NAME Job name $SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID Array task index $SLURM_NNODES Number of nodes $SLURM_NTASKS Total tasks $SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK CPUs per task $SLURM_NODELIST Allocated nodes $SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR Submission directory $SLURM_GPUS_ON_NODE GPUs on this node

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• GPU Jobs — requesting GPUs and choosing partitions
  • Analysis and Data Processing — CPU partition for data work
  • Job Arrays — parameter sweeps
  • Monitoring Jobs — checking status and resource usage
  • Job Troubleshooting — common failures

docs.title: Job Troubleshooting
docs.text: Common job problems and how to fix them.

docs.title: Common Failures
docs.text: Problem Likely Cause Solution Job stays PENDING Resources unavailable or fairshare is low Run squeue -j JOBID to see the reason code. Reduce resource requests or wait for fairshare recovery. PENDING reason: Priority Other jobs have higher priority Your fairshare may be temporarily low. Wait, or reduce your resource request. PENDING reason: Resources Not enough nodes/GPUs available Check sinfo -p PARTITION for capacity. Reduce --nodes or --gpus, or try a different partition. PENDING reason: QOSMaxJobsPerUserLimit Too many devel jobs Devel partitions are limited to 4 concurrent jobs. Wait for one to finish or cancel one. error: Batch job submission failed: Invalid account Wrong --account value Use your institution's account: bu, hu, mit, neu, umass, uml, umb, umassd, umassmed, yale, ma, aihub. See Requesting Access for the account matching your institution. error: Unable to allocate resources: Invalid partition Partition name misspelled Valid partitions: cpu, rtx-batch, rtx-devel, b200-batch, b200-devel. Job killed: OUT_OF_MEMORY Program exceeded requested memory Increase --mem or --mem-per-cpu. Check actual usage with sacct -j JOBID --format=MaxRSS. Job killed: TIMEOUT Wall time too short Increase --time. Check how long similar jobs took with sacct. Consider checkpointing. FAILED with exit code 1 Your program crashed Check the output and error files. Debug interactively: salloc --partition=rtx-devel --gpus=1 --cpus-per-task=4 --mem=16G --time=01:00:00 --account=ACCOUNT_NAME GPUs not visible (nvidia-smi shows nothing) Missing --gpus or wrong partition Verify you requested GPUs (--gpus=N or --gres=gpu:N) and submitted to a GPU partition. CUDA error: no CUDA-capable device GPU not allocated or CUDA not loaded Check nvidia-smi first. If GPUs show up, load the CUDA module: module load cuda. Node failure (NODE_FAIL state) Hardware issue on the compute node Not your fault. Resubmit the job — Slurm will assign a different node.

docs.title: Check Output Files
docs.text:
The first thing to do when a job fails is read the output and error files:

$ cat JOBNAME-JOBID.out
$ cat JOBNAME-JOBID.err

docs.title: Debug Interactively
docs.text:
Request a short interactive session on the same partition and reproduce the issue:

$ salloc --partition=rtx-devel --gpus=1 --cpus-per-task=4 --mem=16G --time=01:00:00 --account=ACCOUNT_NAME

Then run your commands step by step to isolate the failure.

docs.title: Check Resource Usage
docs.text:
If your job completed but the results seem wrong, check what resources it actually used:

$ sacct -j JOBID --format=JobID,Elapsed,MaxRSS,MaxVMSize,State,ExitCode

If MaxRSS is close to your --mem request, the job may have been silently constrained. Request more memory.

docs.title: Check Module Environment
docs.text:
If software behaves differently in a batch job than interactively, ensure your job script starts with module purge and explicitly loads everything it needs:

module purge
module load cuda python

Do not rely on modules loaded in your login session — batch jobs start with a clean environment.

docs.title: Getting More Help
docs.text:
If you cannot resolve the issue:

  1. Note the job ID, partition, error message, and what you tried
  2. Contact your institution's RC team — see Getting Help

docs.text:
• Slurm Basics — correct directives and syntax
  • Monitoring Jobs — checking job status and resources
  • GPU Jobs — GPU-specific troubleshooting
  • Getting Help — support channels

docs.title: Compilers
docs.text: System compilers are available on AICR login nodes for building software from source.

docs.title: Available Compilers
docs.text: Compiler Command Language GCC gcc C G++ g++ C++ GFortran gfortran Fortran NVCC nvcc (via CUDA module) CUDA C/C++

docs.title: Compiling a C Program
docs.text:
$ gcc -O2 -o my_program my_program.c
$ ./my_program

docs.title: Compiling CUDA Code
docs.text:
Load the CUDA module first:

$ module load cuda
$ nvcc -o my_gpu_program my_gpu_program.cu

docs.title: Building from Source
docs.text:
Many research software packages follow the configure/make pattern:

$ tar -xzf package.tar.gz
$ cd package/
$ ./configure --prefix=/home/$USER/software/package
$ make -j8
$ make install

Add the install location to your PATH:

$ export PATH=/home/$USER/software/package/bin:$PATH

Tip

For large builds, use scratch storage and compile in an interactive session on a compute node:

$ salloc --partition=cpu --cpus-per-task=32 --mem=64G --time=02:00:00 --account=ACCOUNT_NAME

docs.title: CMake
docs.text:
For CMake-based projects:

$ mkdir build && cd build
$ cmake .. -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/home/$USER/software/package
$ make -j8
$ make install

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Modules (Lmod) — check if the software is already available as a module
  • Containers (Apptainer) — for complex dependency chains, a container may be simpler

docs.title: Containers (Apptainer)
docs.text:
Both Apptainer and Singularity CE are available on all AICR nodes. They are largely compatible — most .sif container images and definition files work with either. Containers let you package a complete software environment — OS, libraries, application — into a single .sif file that runs without root privileges.

Note

Apptainer runs without setuid by default on AICR. The --fakeroot flag works for building containers, but some advanced networking flags may not be available.

docs.title: Why Containers?
docs.text:
• Reproducibility: The same container produces the same results everywhere
  • Complex dependencies: Bundle software stacks that are difficult to install (specific CUDA + PyTorch + custom libraries)
  • Portability: Build once, run on AICR, your laptop, or another cluster

docs.title: Running a Container
docs.text:
$ apptainer exec my_container.sif python train.py

Run with GPU support:

$ apptainer exec --nv my_container.sif python train.py

The --nv flag binds the host NVIDIA drivers and libraries into the container, giving your application GPU access.

docs.title: Running Containers in Jobs
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=container_job
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=32G
#SBATCH --time=08:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME
#SBATCH --output=%x-%j.out

apptainer exec --nv /scratch/$USER/containers/my_app.sif python train.py

docs.title: Pulling Pre-Built Images
docs.text:
Pull containers from Docker Hub or NVIDIA NGC:

$ apptainer pull pytorch_latest.sif docker://nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:24.01-py3
$ apptainer pull ubuntu.sif docker://ubuntu:22.04

Tip

Store .sif files on /scratch/$USER. They can be large (5–20 GB) and are easy to rebuild.

docs.title: Building Containers
docs.text:
Build from a definition file using --fakeroot (no root privileges needed):

$ apptainer build --fakeroot my_app.sif my_app.def

Warning

Build containers on a compute node via salloc, not on a login node. Container builds are CPU- and memory-intensive.

docs.title: Example Definition File
docs.text:
Bootstrap: docker
From: pytorch/pytorch:2.2.0-cuda12.1-cudnn8-runtime

%post
    pip install transformers datasets accelerate

%runscript
    python "$@"

Build and run:

$ apptainer build --fakeroot my_pytorch.sif my_pytorch.def
$ apptainer exec --nv my_pytorch.sif python train.py

docs.title: Binding Directories
docs.text:
By default, Apptainer mounts your home directory. To access other paths, use --bind:

$ apptainer exec --bind /scratch/$USER:/data my_container.sif python process.py --input /data/dataset.csv

This makes /scratch/$USER available inside the container at /data.

docs.title: MPI with Containers
docs.text:
For multi-node jobs, use srun with PMIx to launch the container:

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=mpi_container
#SBATCH --partition=b200-batch
#SBATCH --nodes=2
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=8
#SBATCH --gpus-per-node=8
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME

srun --mpi=pmix apptainer exec --nv my_mpi_app.sif ./my_application

The host InfiniBand stack (UCX, RDMA libraries) is available inside the container for high-performance inter-node communication.

docs.text:

docs.title: Modules (Lmod)
docs.text: AICR uses Lmod to manage software. Modules configure your shell so the right compilers, libraries, and tools are available without conflicting.

docs.title: Finding Software
docs.text:
$ module avail              # list all available modules
$ module spider cuda        # search for a package and show all versions

module spider also shows dependencies you need to load first.

docs.title: Loading and Unloading
docs.text:
$ module load cuda          # load a module
$ module load cuda/VERSION  # load a specific version
$ module list               # see what's loaded
$ module unload cuda        # remove a module
$ module purge              # reset to clean state

docs.title: Modules in Job Scripts
docs.text:
Always load modules inside your job script. Compute nodes start with a clean environment.

#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=my_job
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME

module purge
module load cuda python

python train.py

Tip

Start every job script with module purge followed by explicit module load commands. This ensures reproducible environments.

docs.title: AICR Module Hierarchy
docs.text:
Modules are organized under /apps:

  • Core AICR modules at /apps/aicr/modulefiles — shared across all institutions. Includes Miniforge, GCC, CUDA, CUDA-aware MPI, Nvhpc, NetCDF/HDF5, and more.
  • Institution-specific modules at /apps/<institution>/modulefiles — maintained by each institution's RC team, readable by all AICR users. Your institution's modules are loaded by default via a StdEnv module.

Module naming follows the convention: softwarename/version (e.g., cuda/12.4, miniforge/24.1).

You can load another institution's modules if they have software you need:

$ module use /apps/mit/modulefiles    # add MIT's modules to your search path
$ module avail                         # now shows both AICR and MIT modules

docs.title: Centrally Installed Software
docs.text:
The following are available as core AICR modules in /apps/aicr:

Module Description miniforge Conda package manager (Miniforge distribution) gcc GNU Compiler Collection cuda NVIDIA CUDA toolkit openmpi CUDA-aware MPI library nvhpc NVIDIA HPC SDK (compilers + libraries) netcdf NetCDF data format library hdf5 HDF5 data format library

docs.title: Requesting Software
docs.text:
If you need software that isn't available as a module:

  1. Install it yourself in a Conda environment (module load miniforge first), Apptainer container, or your home directory
  2. Contact your institution's RC team to request an installation in your institution's /apps/<institution> tree

docs.text:

docs.title: Python and Conda
docs.text: Python 3.12 is installed on all AICR nodes as part of the base system. For managing packages and isolated environments, use Conda or Python's built-in venv.

docs.title: System Python
docs.text:
Python 3.12 is available without loading a module:

$ python3 --version
Python 3.12.x

Warning

Do not install packages into the system Python with pip install outside of a virtual environment. This can conflict with system libraries. Always use a Conda environment or venv.

docs.title: Conda
docs.text:
Miniforge (a Conda distribution) is available as a centrally installed module:

$ module load miniforge
$ conda --version

This gives you access to conda and mamba for creating isolated environments.

docs.title: Creating Environments
docs.text:
$ conda create --name myenv python=3.12
$ conda activate myenv
$ conda install numpy pandas scikit-learn

Large environments

If your environment is large (ML frameworks can be 10+ GB), create it on scratch to avoid filling your 100 GB home quota:

$ conda create --prefix /scratch/$USER/conda-envs/myenv python=3.12
$ conda activate /scratch/$USER/conda-envs/myenv

docs.title: Managing Environments
docs.text:
$ conda env list                    # list all environments
$ conda activate myenv              # activate
$ conda deactivate                  # deactivate
$ conda env remove --name myenv     # delete an environment
$ conda env export > environment.yml  # export for reproducibility
$ conda clean --all                 # free cache space

docs.title: Conda in Job Scripts
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda_job
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME

module purge

eval "$(conda shell.bash hook)"
conda activate myenv

python train.py

docs.title: pip and venv
docs.text:
For lightweight setups, use Python's built-in venv:

$ python3 -m venv ~/myenv
$ source ~/myenv/bin/activate
$ pip install torch torchvision

Deactivate with:

$ deactivate

docs.title: uv — A Faster Alternative to pip
docs.text: uv is a drop-in replacement for pip and venv that is significantly faster — installs that take minutes with pip often complete in seconds with uv. It is written in Rust and resolves dependencies more efficiently.

docs.title: Installing uv
docs.text:
uv is a single binary with no dependencies:

$ curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

Or install via pip into an existing environment:

$ pip install uv

docs.title: Creating Environments with uv
docs.text:
uv can create and manage virtual environments directly:

$ uv venv /scratch/$USER/envs/myproject
$ source /scratch/$USER/envs/myproject/bin/activate
$ uv pip install torch torchvision numpy pandas

docs.title: Using uv as a pip Drop-In
docs.text:
Within any activated virtual environment, use uv pip wherever you would use pip:

$ uv pip install transformers datasets accelerate
$ uv pip install -r requirements.txt
$ uv pip list
$ uv pip freeze > requirements.txt

docs.title: uv in Job Scripts
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=uv_job
#SBATCH --partition=rtx-batch
#SBATCH --gpus=1
#SBATCH --time=04:00:00
#SBATCH --account=ACCOUNT_NAME

source /scratch/$USER/envs/myproject/bin/activate

python train.py

Tip

uv is especially useful when installing large ML frameworks (PyTorch, JAX, TensorFlow) — the dependency resolution and download are much faster than pip. It respects the same requirements.txt format, so existing workflows work without changes.

docs.title: Jupyter
docs.text:
Jupyter notebooks can be run through OnDemand (if available) or via an SSH tunnel:

  1. In a job script or interactive session, start Jupyter:

    $ jupyter lab --no-browser --port=8888 --ip=0.0.0.0

  2. On your local machine, set up an SSH tunnel:

    $ ssh -L 8888:NODE_NAME:8888 USERNAME@login.aicr.ai

  3. Open http://localhost:8888 in your browser.

docs.title: Cache Management
docs.text:
Conda, pip, and HuggingFace caches can quickly fill your 100 GB home quota. Redirect them to scratch by adding these lines to your ~/.bashrc:

export CONDA_PKGS_DIRS=/scratch/$USER/.conda/pkgs
export PIP_CACHE_DIR=/scratch/$USER/.cache/pip
export HF_HOME=/scratch/$USER/.cache/huggingface

See Managing Quotas for detailed cleanup strategies and more cache locations to watch.
